title: Wet-felted pendant necklace
subtitle: A small round pendant in merino on a leather or ribbon cord
excerpt: Wet felt a small round disc of merino, reinforce it with a second layer in a contrasting colour, and fit it onto a cord to make a wearable pendant. The finished disc is about 3 cm across when dry.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from contemporary British felt jewellery practice. Wet-felted pendants and wearables are a standard beginner project in UK felting workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre prepared in a long ribbon form with the fibres roughly parallel. For pendants, small wisps of roving are the correct scale to build up a compact flat disc.
The final working stage of wet felting when the piece is rubbed firmly to shrink and consolidate the fibres. Fulling gives the finished felt a dense, firm texture.

content.text: This pendant is made by building two round layers of

on the bamboo mat, wetting them down, and felting them together into a single dense disc. A cord is threaded through a hole in the top made with a blunt needle while the felt is still slightly damp. Plan for a raw starting size of about 6 cm across, which will shrink to about 3 cm when fully finished.

items.name: Leather cord, ribbon, or waxed cotton (necklace length)
Blunt knitting needle or pencil (for piercing hole)

content.text: Pull tiny wisps of base colour merino and arrange them in overlapping layers in a rough circle about 6 cm across on the bamboo mat. Use three layers, each turned 60 degrees from the last. Keep the layers thin; you are building a pendant, not a vessel wall.

Drizzle a small amount of warm water over the stack with your fingers until it is saturated. Rub a tiny amount of soap across the surface. Cover with a piece of net and rub with flat-hand circular pressure for 5 minutes. Lift the net, flip the disc, replace the net, and rub the other side for another 3 minutes.

While the base disc is still damp, lay thin wisps of the contrasting colour merino in a simple pattern on the surface. A swirl, a spiral, or abstract patches all work well. Cover with the net and rub lightly to integrate the surface colour. The contrasting layer should stay visible; do not over-rub at this stage.

by pressing the disc firmly between both palms and squeezing steadily for 2 minutes. Roll it in the bamboo mat and roll back and forth for 1 minute. The disc should shrink to approximately 3 cm across. Trim the edge with sharp scissors if you prefer a precise circle. Rinse in cool water and press dry in a towel.

While the disc is still slightly damp, push a blunt knitting needle or pencil through the top of the disc from front to back to create a cord hole about 5 mm from the top edge. Enlarge it gently to approximately 3 mm across by rotating the needle. Leave to dry fully with the hole open. Once dry, thread the cord through the hole and tie a secure knot at each end.

items.problem: The cord hole closes up as the disc dries
items.cause: The disc was not dried with something holding the hole open
items.fix: Push a matchstick or small wooden skewer through the hole and leave it in place until the disc is completely dry. Remove the skewer and the hole remains open.

items.problem: The surface colour has disappeared into the base
items.cause: Over-rubbing during the surface-colour step pushed the merino too deep
items.fix: Apply the surface colour with lighter pressure next time, or add it after the initial felting is complete and the base is already firm. A firm base lets the surface colour sit on top rather than sinking through.
